participants.name: Mei
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-q5bhbaa2
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-q5bhbaa2
participants.professional_persona: Mei Ling is a career‑switcher currently enrolled in the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP) after a decade in retail management. She is passionate about using AI to improve customer experience for small businesses.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Strong domain knowledge in retail operations and customer analytics; gaining hands‑on experience in machine learning and natural language processing.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: She aspires to become an AI solutions architect for the retail sector, helping neighbourhood shops adopt AI tools that are safe and trusted.

participants.name: A (0s9ao7)
participants.professional_persona: A robotics AI engineer at a startup that builds autonomous mobile robots for warehouse logistics. He specializes in reinforcement learning and sensor fusion to enable real-time navigation in dynamic environments.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Solid background in ROS, SLAM, computer vision, and control systems; proficient in C++ and Python; experience with simulation environments (Gazebo, MuJoCo).
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Hopes to scale his startup's robots for use in Singapore's port and airport operations, and to contribute to Singapore's positioning as a smart logistics hub.

participants.name: Dr. (dtrg0f)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Nurul Aisyah Binte Abdullah is a Senior Research Fellow at Singapore Management University, investigating fairness and bias in AI systems. She collaborates closely with government agencies to audit public-sector AI deployments for equity.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expertise in algorithmic fairness, causal inference, and responsible AI frameworks. Experienced in designing bias-mitigation techniques that are practical for enterprise use.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Seeks to shape Singapore's national AI ethics guidelines and influence global standards. Aspires to lead a dedicated centre for responsible AI research.

participants.name: Tan
participants.professional_persona: Tan Keng Leong is a self-taught AI researcher who now leads the AI Lab at a large logistics firm, DHL Singapore. He started his career as a data analyst and built his expertise through company-led training programmes like TeSA’s AIAP.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Practical skills in reinforcement learning for route optimization, predictive maintenance, and demand forecasting. Proficient in deploying models on edge devices within warehouse environments.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to demonstrate that non-traditional career paths can produce top-tier AI talent. Aims to mentor career-switchers and advocate for skills-based hiring in AI roles.

participants.name: Dr. (jns746)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Priya Shankar is a postdoctoral researcher at Singapore University of Technology and Design, focusing on generative AI for architectural design. She originally moved to Singapore on a PhD scholarship and now contributes to the 100 Experiments programme with building-industry partners.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Skilled in generative adversarial networks, 3D modelling, and human-in-the-loop design systems. Combines AI creativity with structural engineering constraints to produce viable blueprints.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to revolutionize the construction sector by making generative design accessible to small architecture firms. Hopes to secure a tenure-track faculty position in Singapore.
participants.education_level: PhD in Architecture and Artificial Intelligence

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI apprentice with a retail background, I see the government's commitment to AI R&D as substantial. The policy states over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore, which I take as the total funding. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is explicitly stated. For compute, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours based on the need to support multiple experiments and training, though this is not directly stated. For trainers, I estimate 50 personnel, considering the need to guide enterprises and apprentices like me across various sectors.

The resource logic clearly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', which I take as the max co-funding per project.
Given the ambition to generate 100 use cases and support many experiments, I estimate 500,000 GPU hours as a reasonable compute allocation, though not explicitly stated.
With a target to boost the AI practitioner pool to 15,000 and run programmes like AIAP, I estimate 50 trainers to provide adequate guidance, based on typical trainer-to-trainee ratios.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group discussed that the S$500 million funding and 50 trainers enable a substantial outreach effort, with causal mechanisms linking outreach sessions to diverse sector engagement, matchmaking events to researcher-enterprise pairing, training workshops to practical AI upskilling, and onboarding assists to platform adoption support. Consensus emerged on researcher_matchmaking_events (10), training_workshops_held (20), and platform_onboarding_assists (90), with most participants agreeing on these numbers. Disagreement centered on enterprise_outreach_sessions, where estimates ranged from 30 to 40, reflecting a tension between prioritizing quality over quantity versus ensuring broad sector coverage. The central tendency for outreach sessions was 35, with a range of 30–40; matchmaking events were 10 (range 10–12); training workshops were 20 (range 20–25); and onboarding assists were 90 (range 80–100).

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding, S$330,000 co-funding cap per project, 500,000 GPU hours, and 50 trainers, I estimate that around 60 100E projects will be approved in 2023. This is based on the typical project cost and the need to spread funding across diverse sectors like logistics, manufacturing, and healthcare. With 35 outreach sessions and 10 matchmaking events, many enterprises will be engaged, but not all will lead to approved projects. For AI prototypes, I expect about 45 to be successfully built, as some projects may face technical challenges or delays. The 20 training workshops and 90 onboarding assists will support prototype development, but the complexity of AI solutions means not all approved projects will yield a prototype in the same year. For trainees, I estimate 80 will complete the programme, given the 9-month duration and the 50 trainers available to guide them. This aligns with the scale of the AIAP and the need for hands-on mentorship. Finally, I estimate 50 enterprises will have at least one AI solution developed through the programme, as some prototypes may not be fully deployed or adopted by the enterprise within the year. My estimates are grounded in practical experience with logistics sector resource constraints and the need for quality over quantity.
evidence: With S$500 million funding and S$330,000 co-funding per project, the maximum number of fully funded projects is around 1,515, but practical constraints like trainer capacity and outreach limit approvals to about 60 in the first year.
Based on my experience deploying AI in logistics, about 75% of approved projects typically yield a working prototype within a year, so 45 prototypes from 60 approved projects is realistic.
The 9-month AIAP duration and 50 trainers suggest a trainee-to-trainer ratio of about 1.6 trainees per trainer per cohort, yielding roughly 80 completions in 2023.
Given that some prototypes may not be adopted by enterprises due to integration challenges, I estimate 50 enterprises will have a solution, reflecting a 83% adoption rate among those with prototypes.

initial_posts.narrative: As a postdoc working on generative AI for architecture, I see the prior phases providing a strong foundation: S$500 million funding, 50 trainers, 35 outreach sessions, 10 matchmaking events, 20 training workshops, and 90 onboarding assists. From the Outputs phase, 60 projects were approved, 45 prototypes built, 80 trainees completed, and 50 enterprises got a solution. Now at Outcomes, I estimate that about half of the 45 prototypes (30) will reach production deployment in 2023, as construction and other sectors face integration delays. The AI practitioner pool is growing but likely around 12,000, short of the 15,000 target, because the 80 new trainees and other upskilling efforts add incrementally to the existing base. For generative AI use cases, the AI Trailblazers programme aims for 100 in 100 days, but I think 80 is more realistic given onboarding and sector-specific adaptation. Enterprise AI adoption rate is around 25%, as many firms are still experimenting with prototypes and not yet fully integrating AI into operations. My estimates are grounded in my experience with the slow pace of adoption in the construction sector and the policy's ambitious targets.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group reached full consensus on all impact estimates, predicting Singapore's global AI readiness rank improves to 5th, AI sector GDP share reaches 3.5%, public AI trust score hits 65%, and AI talent retention rate is 75%. Key causal mechanisms included the comprehensive NAIS 2.0 strategy, S$500 million funding, 60 approved projects, 30 deployed solutions, 80 generative AI use cases, and 25% enterprise adoption driving improved readiness and economic contribution. Public trust was seen as bolstered by successful deployments and government transparency, though tempered by concerns about job displacement, ethics, and fairness, especially among vulnerable groups and small businesses. AI talent retention was supported by hands-on programme experience and startup opportunities, but some outflow to global tech hubs was acknowledged. No numeric disagreements occurred, though minor qualitative nuances were noted regarding trust levels in specific sectors.
